nameMeanings.name: BRANDON
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from any of various places called Brandon, in Durham, Norfolk, Suffolk, Warwickshire, and elsewhere. Most are named with Old English brōm ‘broom, gorse’ + dūn ‘hill’. One in Lincolnshire, however, may be named after the Brant River, on which it stands; Ekwall derives the river name from Old English brant ‘steep’, presumably with reference to its steep banks. This surname was known in Ireland early enough to have a Gaelicized form de Breandún.
Irish (Kerry): Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Breandáin ‘son of Breandán’, a rare Kerry surname from the Latinized form of the name of the local saint, Brendan (Brendanus from Bréanainn).
French: from the Old French oblique case of the personal name Brando, of ancient Germanic origin (see Brand 1).

variants.name: Brand
meanings: English, Scottish, North German, Scandinavian, Dutch, and French (mainly Alsace and Lorraine): from the ancient Germanic personal name Brando, Old Norse Brandr, a short form of various compound names containing the element brand ‘sword’ (a derivative of brinnan ‘to flash’), of which the best known is Hildebrand . There is placename evidence for Brant(a) as an Old English personal name; however, the Middle English personal name Brand was probably introduced to England from Old Norse.
German: variant of   Brandt   1 and, in North America, (also) an altered form of this.
Swedish and Danish: ornamental name or nickname from brand ‘fire’.

variants.name: Brandom
meanings: English and Jewish (Sephardic): variant of   Brandon  .

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: William BRANDON I was born on 9 January 1355, in North Cray, Kent, England. He married Anne de Bacon in 1381, in North Cray, Kent, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died after 1405, in Kings Lynn, Norfolk, England, United Kingdom.

ko.lifeSummary: William BRANDON I(은)는 1355년 1월 9일 영국 켄트 노르스 크라이에서 태어났다. 그는 1381년 영국 잉글랜드 켄트 노르스 크라이에서 Anne de Bacon(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1405년 이후 영국 잉글랜드 노르폴크 킨그스 린에서 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: William BRANDON I родился 9 января 1355 г. в местности норт креи, кэнт, Англия. Он женился на женщине по имени Anne de Bacon в 1381 г. в местности норт креи, кэнт, Англия, Великобритания. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын. Он умер после 1405 г. в местности кингс линн, норфолк, Англия, Великобритания.
